ocar.opportunity: The neural circuits that control coordinated limb movements in animals are poorly understood at the synaptic level, requiring connectomic analysis of motor control networks in the ventral nerve cord.
ocar.challenge: The ventral nerve cord of Drosophila contains densely packed neuronal processes controlling multiple body segments and requires large-volume serial section EM acquisition with automated transmission electron microscopy to achieve adequate coverage.
ocar.action: The authors used automated transmission electron microscopy to acquire serial section EM images of the Drosophila adult ventral nerve cord, then performed semi-automated connectomic reconstruction of motor control circuits including motor neurons, premotor interneurons, and descending command neurons.
ocar.resolution: The reconstruction revealed the synaptic organization of circuits controlling walking and grooming behaviors, identifying key premotor interneurons and their connectivity to motor neuron pools. This provides the first large-scale connectomic description of the Drosophila locomotor system.
ocar.future_work: Connecting the reconstructed circuits to specific behavioral states through optogenetics and behavior, and extending the reconstruction to the full ventral nerve cord, are important next steps.

summaries.beginner: Fruit flies walk and groom themselves using precisely coordinated leg movements. This paper mapped the nerve circuits that control these movements in the fly's spinal cord equivalent, using a robotic electron microscope to image thousands of thin slices.
summaries.intermediate: The authors used automated TEM to image and reconstruct motor control circuits in the Drosophila adult ventral nerve cord (VNC). The connectome encompasses motor neurons, premotor interneurons, and descending inputs, revealing the layered organization of locomotor circuit control. Key circuit motifs such as reciprocal inhibition and convergent premotor drive were identified.
summaries.advanced: This study extends Drosophila connectomics to the VNC using high-throughput automated TEM, enabling reconstruction of the motor control hierarchy from descending command neurons to motor neuron pools. The analysis reveals convergence of multiple interneuron classes onto shared motor neurons and identifies candidate pattern-generating circuits for walking. Caveats include incomplete VNC coverage at the time of publication and the challenge of functionally validating the identified circuit motifs without activity data matched to the EM volume.

discussion_prompts: How does the organization of premotor interneuron connectivity to motor neuron pools in Drosophila VNC compare to the organization of spinal cord motor circuits in vertebrates?
What circuit motifs identified in this reconstruction are consistent with central pattern generator models, and how could the connectome be used to constrain CPG models?
How did automated TEM improve throughput and quality compared to conventional serial section EM, and what are its remaining limitations for large-scale VNC reconstruction?
